perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall apply to said PCQ and ABW and all of their subsidiaries and related companies.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment should generally apply to said PCQ and ABW, with reasonable efforts made to include their subsidiaries and related companies.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall apply' to 'should generally apply' and adding 'with reasonable efforts'. This makes the application of the amendment to subsidiaries and related companies discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles of Enforceability and Third-Party Beneficiaries
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the application to subsidiaries and related companies subject to 'reasonable efforts,' the amendment introduces uncertainty about which entities are actually bound. This ambiguity could hinder enforcement, especially if 'reasonable efforts' is not clearly defined, potentially undermining the intended beneficiaries' rights under the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: third party beneficiary | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you third party beneficiary A third party beneficiary is a person benefiting from a contract made between two other parties, where the two contracting parties intended to benefit the third party beneficiary. The third party beneficiary is not a party to the contract, but has rights under the contract since it was made with an intent to benefit them. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract law Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-318. Third Party Beneficiaries of Warranties Express or Implied. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-318. Third Party Beneficiaries of Warranties Express or Implied. [ Note: If this Act is introduced in the Congress of the United States this section should be omitted. (States to select one alternative.) ] Alternative A A seller's warranty whether express or implied extends to any natural person who is in the family or household of his buyer or who is a guest in his home if it is reasonable to expect that such person may use, consume or be affected by the goods and who is injured in person by breach of the warranty. A seller may not exclude or limit the operation of this section. Alternative B A seller's warranty whether express or implied extends to any natural person who may reasonably be expected to use, consume or be affected by the goods and who is injured in person by breach of the warranty. A seller may not exclude or limit the operation of this section. Alternative C A seller's warranty whether express or implied extends to any person who may reasonably be expected to use, consume or be affected by the goods and who is injured by breach of the warranty. A seller may not exclude or limit the operation of this section with respect to injury to the person of an individual to whom the warranty extends. [ Note: As amended in 1966. ] Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall apply' to 'should generally apply, with reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation on the contract's application to subsidiaries. The original text mandates application, while the modified text introduces discretion. While the scraped snippets do not directly address the issue of subsidiaries or 'reasonable efforts', the core legal principle at play is the introduction of ambiguity into a previously clear obligation, creating the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, as defined in the Perturbation Definitions.
